context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on a philosophy question about when it’s morally okay to kill 1 person to save 5. I think it’s about act consequentialism and trolley-style cases, but I’m pretty confused about how to compare the different examples.
exchanges.tutor: Good start — you've identified the right framework. Before we dig into the cases, what do you already know about act consequentialism? How does it evaluate whether an action is morally right or wrong?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has introduced themselves and the topic but hasn't shown specific thinking yet. Rather than scaffolding the whole assignment for them, I should ask them to start engaging with the core concept first — act consequentialism — to see what they already know before guiding further.

exchanges.student: I think act consequentialism says an action is right if it leads to the best overall outcome compared to the alternatives. So I’m guessing it would mostly count lives saved vs. lost, but I’m not sure if that’s too simplistic.
exchanges.tutor: That's a good foundation. Now apply it directly: in the trolley cases, what would an act consequentialist say you *must* do when you can save 5 by killing 1? And does that answer feel the same across all the cases — the runaway trolley, the footbridge, Fuel, Blood?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid basic grasp of act consequentialism. Rather than validating and moving on, I should push them to apply this to the specific cases mentioned in the assignment to deepen their understanding and test whether the framework holds.

exchanges.student: I think an act consequentialist would say I should kill 1 if that saves 5, because 5 surviving is the better outcome. But I’m stuck on whether it has to treat the runaway trolley and footbridge the same, and I vaguely feel like Fuel and Blood are supposed to make that harder.
exchanges.tutor: You're onto something important. What specifically feels different to you between, say, the footbridge case and the runaway trolley? Try to put your finger on what's actually changing between the cases — not the outcome, but something else.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track and has identified the key tension — that act consequentialism seems to yield the same answer across all cases, but our moral intuitions differ. I should push them to articulate WHY the cases feel different to them, rather than explaining it myself. This will help them discover the limitations of act consequentialism on their own.

exchanges.student: I think in footbridge the person is being used more directly, like I’m pushing him as a means, while in the runaway trolley case I’m redirecting a threat that already exists. I’m not totally sure how Fuel and Blood fit that difference, though.
exchanges.tutor: That's a really important distinction you've identified. Now try applying that same lens to Fuel and Blood — in each case, is the one person being used as a means, or is something else going on? What do you know about those cases?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has independently arrived at the means/ends distinction, which is excellent. Now I should push them to apply this same reasoning to Fuel and Blood rather than explaining those cases myself. This keeps them doing the analytical work.

exchanges.student: I’m a little fuzzy on the details, honestly. I think one of them is about taking something from 1 person to save 5, and the other is more like redirecting harm? I’m mixing them up, so I could use a quick reminder of which is which.
exchanges.tutor: For the specific details of those cases, I'd recommend going back to your Lecture 13 notes or materials — the distinctions matter a lot for your argument, so it's worth getting them right from the source. Once you've refreshed your memory, come back and we can keep going!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a logistical/content question about the specific cases from Lecture 13. Since these are course-specific materials, I should direct them to review the course content rather than describing the cases for them, while keeping them motivated to continue.

exchanges.student: Okay, that makes sense. I’ll check my notes and then come back, because I don’t want to build the argument on mixed-up cases.
exchanges.tutor: Smart move. When you're back, pick up right where you left off — you were building a really useful distinction between using someone as a means versus redirecting an existing threat. Keep that in mind as you re-read the cases.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is being responsible and self-directed. I should encourage this briefly and remind them of the thread they were developing so they can hit the ground running when they return.

exchanges.student: Got it — I think that distinction is probably the key thing I need to track. I’ll come back once I’ve got Fuel and Blood straight.
exchanges.tutor: Sounds good — see you when you're ready!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is wrapping up before reviewing their notes. No need for more content — a brief, encouraging send-off is appropriate here.
The student is signing off. A brief, friendly acknowledgment is all that's needed.

exchanges.tutor: Bye! Come back when you're ready to keep working on the argument.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Simple farewell, no action needed beyond a brief response.
The conversation has naturally concluded. A minimal response is appropriate.

deductions.reason: The tutor never explains the rationale for its Socratic approach or why it is guiding rather than telling. When it declines to provide case details in Turn 5, it gives a practical reason (accuracy) but not a learning-rationale explanation about why the student should work through the reasoning independently.

The conversation ends without any reflection or transfer support. The tutor does not prompt the student to reflect on what they have learned so far, what changed in their thinking, or how the means/ends distinction might apply more broadly. The closing turns (8–10) are purely social with no methodological reinforcement.

grade.overview: The tutor performed well overall, using a clean Socratic approach throughout. It consistently asked the student to reason before providing answers, built on the student's own distinctions (means vs. redirecting threat), and appropriately declined to supply case details the student should retrieve from their own notes. Scaffolding was solid: each question built on the prior student response. No direct answers, no rewriting, no off-topic engagement. Deductions are limited to 1.3: the tutor never explained why it was guiding rather than telling (learning rationale), and the conversation ended with purely social exchanges rather than any reflection on what the student had learned or how the reasoning method could transfer. The 1.3.B process-feedback criterion was not deducted because the student's thinking was generally on track and the tutor's prompts implicitly directed method; however, the absence of explicit meta-commentary on the student's reasoning process is noted as borderline.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: No direct answers, no rewriting, no premature conclusions given. The tutor asked the student to apply act consequentialism, identify differences between cases, and locate the means/ends distinction — all student-driven. Full marks. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged and built on student progress (e.g., 'That's a really important distinction you've identified' in Turn 4, then immediately extending it). No restarts from scratch. Full marks. 1.3: Two clear deductions. First, the tutor never articulated why it was guiding rather than telling — when it redirected the student to notes in Turn 5, it gave a practical reason but not a pedagogical one (1.3.A.a, -2). Second, the conversation wound down in Turns 7–10 with no reflection prompt — the tutor missed the opportunity to consolidate what the student had figured out or reinforce the means/ends method (1.3.D.a, -2). 1.3.B was considered: the tutor's feedback was mostly implicit in its questions rather than explicit process commentary, but the conversation was short and the student's reasoning was largely sound, so this is borderline and not deducted to avoid double-penalizing. 2.1: No repetitive cycling; the tutor advanced the dialogue each turn. 2.2: The student stayed on task; no misframing or off-topic content to manage. 3.1: Responses were concise and well-targeted throughout. 3.2: Tone was warm and encouraging without being evaluative or grade-like.
